text.text-2: Building Family Wealth and Cultural Continuity on Country
text.text-3: Traditional Owners of Loves Creek Station and Oonchiumpa Consultancy and Services
text.text-4: Supported by A Curious Tractor. A ten-year pathway for intergenerational opportunity, cultural leadership, and community-led enterprise in and around Alice Springs.
text.text-6: We acknowledge the Traditional Owners of Atnarpa and Loves Creek Station, and we pay our respects to Elders past and present. We are Oonchiumpa Consultancy and Services, owned and operated by Arrernte Traditional Owners of Mbantua (Alice Springs) and Loves Creek (Atnarpa), and this proposal is our commitment to strengthen culture, community, and family through our own leadership.
text.text-9: Our family legacy carries responsibility and pride. Atnarpa Homestead and surrounding Country hold both the weight of history and the strength of survival, and we continue sharing cultural knowledge with care.
text.text-10: "We know what we wanna do on our land, and we know how to get there." - Kristy Bloomfield
text.text-11: We continue this work through cultural authority, practical delivery, and a shared commitment to future generations.
text.text-12: Family history and continuity at Atnarpa
text.text-14: From Pastoralist to Tourism and On-Country Learning
text.text-15: Our ancestors shared knowledge through pastoral life at Loves Creek, including station duties, stock work, and family care under difficult conditions. Today we continue that legacy through cultural tourism, on-Country educational training, and intergenerational learning.
text.text-16: Knowledge sharing from station life to visitor engagement
text.text-17: On-country educational training and youth learning
text.text-19: Land claim recognition and community ceremony
text.text-22: Loves Creek Station was one of the earliest pastoral leases in Central Australia. Land claim recognition enables us to lead our own future at Atnarpa and deliver restoration, activation, and long-term planning under Traditional Owner leadership.
text.text-23: Today, we continue sharing culture with care and invite respectful participation guided by our rules and values.
text.text-24: "Our kids 10 years time, that's our focus at the moment." - Kylie Bloomfield
text.text-27: Our immediate infrastructure focus is the campground conference precinct, centered between the dongas, with supporting links to accommodation, amenities, and cultural gathering space. Homestead-side works remain part of the plan as smaller supporting upgrades staged around this core build.
text.text-28: Campground heart: primary location for conference structure, connected deck network, and cultural gathering zone.
text.text-30: Two 40-foot containers with a roof between can form a fully enclosed and air-conditioned function/training room. The proposed internal footprint is approximately 11m by 6m (66 sqm).
text.text-31: From this core, open-air decking can connect to accommodation blocks, kitchen access, and a central firepit/yarning area to support workshops, conferences, and community gatherings.
text.text-32: Material selection, footings, approvals, and transport to site are treated as key delivery constraints in the staging and quote pathway.
text.text-34: These drawings show how the campground build can be organized on Country: conference core in the center, connected deck circulation, and a central yarning/firepit point with staged links to accommodation and services.
text.text-35: They support package planning from concept through approvals, footings, materials delivery, and on-site sequencing.
text.text-36: Main overlay plan across the full section: conference core, linked deck network, and yarning circle layout.
text.text-37: Concept sketch: covered structure and connection logic between spaces.
text.text-38: Concept sketch: central room, circulation paths, and firepit/yarning area.
text.text-40: This list translates the sketches into practical campground upgrade points, so scope is clear before approvals, procurement, and site mobilization.
text.text-41: Conference Structure: Two 40-foot containers joined with a roof between, fully enclosed and air-conditioned (approximately 11m x 6m / 66 sqm) for functions, conferences, training, and gatherings.
text.text-42: Decking and Circulation: Open-air decking joins the central structure to accommodation and kitchen access, creating one connected communal movement path.
text.text-43: Accommodation Upgrades: Existing dongas are upgraded into three accommodation blocks linked to the conference structure via decking.
text.text-44: Fire Pit and Gathering: A central fire pit / yarning point anchors cultural gathering and story-sharing in the campground heart.
text.text-45: Homestead Supporting Upgrades: Two toilet and shower blocks at the homestead are installed/upgraded through staged reconfiguration of existing infrastructure.
text.text-46: Annotated concept plan showing conference core, deck network, accommodation upgrades, amenities block, and communal fire pit hub.
text.text-47: Opens as a true full-screen drawing view.
text.text-49: These supporting works sit alongside the campground-first build and are staged as practical upgrades around the existing homestead footprint.
text.text-50: Two toilet and shower blocks: installed and upgraded at the homestead location through reconfiguration of old cattle-block infrastructure.
text.text-51: Landscaping upgrades: targeted ground shaping around the homestead and campground interface to define safe movement, access, and usable outdoor areas.
text.text-52: Homestead roof complete: roof restoration has been completed and the site is now ready for safe next stages of development.
text.text-53: Opens as a true full-screen map view.
text.text-55: Current indicative costs (AUD) for the scoped works, based on latest concept planning and remote-site delivery assumptions.
text.text-56: Known current cost total: $221,800 AUD. Dream-plan uplift remains to be confirmed through co-design, technical review, and partner confirmation.
text.text-58: Expanded History, Evidence, and Implementation Context
text.text-59: This appendix holds additional family stories, project evidence, media, and planning detail that sit behind the core send version.
text.text-61: Expanded History and Land Claim Context
text.text-62: Our families have carried knowledge through station life, cultural teaching, and community leadership across generations. Land claim recognition at Loves Creek remains central to how we guide present and future use of Country.
text.text-63: This context informs current project decisions: we are building practical infrastructure that supports cultural authority, youth pathways, and long-term family opportunity.
text.text-64: Land claim and governance context that grounds infrastructure planning decisions.
text.text-67: The previous build week and partnership context is documented in this story from A Curious Tractor. It can sit beside this pitch as supporting evidence of delivery and trust.
text.text-71: These clips show day-to-day work, land care, and family-led activity on station. Videos are click-to-play.
text.text-74: "This week has made a huge impact on our family and creating our own history."
text.text-76: "Growing up here, mate, it's God's country."
text.text-78: "We know what we wanna do on our land, and we know how to get there."
text.text-80: "Our kids 10 years time, that's our focus at the moment."
text.text-82: "It's just good to be coming home, to grow my kids up here."
text.text-84: "What they created here with us this week was unbelievable."
text.text-87: 10-Year Vision: Generational Wealth, Enterprise, and Country (2026-2036)
text.text-88: Our long-term direction is to build intergenerational wealth through family-led enterprise on Country at Atnarpa, while protecting cultural authority and community wellbeing. We are carrying this work forward from the station lease history, including the Hewett family lease period, into a future led by Traditional Owners.
text.text-89: This pathway links infrastructure, programs, training, and governance so young people can move into practical employment, business leadership, and long-term stewardship of land and culture.
text.text-91: Complete design, approvals, and enabling works while setting up the first family-led enterprise operations. Start early youth pathways, on-Country learning delivery, and governance systems that support staged growth.
text.text-93: Deliver and activate the campground conference precinct, connected circulation, and essential service edge so education, events, cultural tourism, and training can operate as one family-led system.
text.text-95: Scale revenue streams, strengthen enterprise capability, and transfer leadership to the next generation through mentoring, ownership pathways, and staged homestead upgrades that support long-term stability.
text.text-99: Cultural learning, storytelling, and identity-strengthening led by Traditional Owners and mentors.
text.text-101: Skill development for employment in hospitality, land care, logistics, and cultural program delivery.
text.text-103: Shared gatherings, intergenerational exchange, and respectful visitor engagement on Country.
text.text-105: Story sharing and locally led offerings that build family income and protect cultural authority.
text.text-106: Function room and gathering space context
text.text-107: Activated event and workshop area concept
text.text-111: Track how many families and young people from Alice Springs and surrounding homelands engage in Oonchiumpa-led workshops, cultural programs, tourism activities, and repeat visits on Country.
text.text-113: Track progression from first participation into training, paid work, mentoring, and enterprise roles so the Bloomfield children and regional youth build leadership, confidence, and practical skills on Country.
text.text-115: Track staged delivery of campground and homestead works, operational readiness, and transition planning through the current Hewett lease period so the next 10 years strengthen Indigenous wealth and create a family-led case study for the NT and Australia.
text.text-118: Atnarpa is a place of memory and possibility. We are seeking partnership that strengthens our capacity to deliver our own vision over the next ten years.
text.text-119: We invite Snow Foundation to co-design the next step with us through a review meeting and site conversation, so scope and delivery can be finalised together in the right way.
text.text-120: "We know what we want to do on our land, and we know how to get there."
text.text-122: Additional Photos of the Sites and Supporting Homestead Upgrades
text.text-123: This appendix shares additional photos of the sites and identifies supporting homestead-side upgrades to be staged after, or alongside, the core campground build packages.
text.text-124: Campsite context image for planning deck, cover, and support-area staging.

text.text-126: Outside view of donger layout and circulation between structures.
